turns.content: Okay I need help. I have a portfolio review coming up on February 20th with an agency that's looking for a freelance subcontractor and my case studies are a MESS. Like embarrassingly outdated. I have six days.

Six days is tight but workable if you're strategic. Let's not try to overhaul everything — let's figure out what needs to happen and in what order. How many case studies do you currently have, and how many does the agency expect to see?

I have like eight on my site but honestly half of them are from two or three years ago and the presentation is weak. The work itself is good but the writeups are lazy — like just a couple images and a sentence. The agency wants to see packaging and branding work specifically. I think showing three or four really strong case studies is better than eight mediocre ones right?

Absolutely — three to four polished case studies will make a much stronger impression than eight thin ones. Quality over quantity, especially for an agency evaluating whether they want to work with you. They want to see your thinking process, not just pretty pictures.

Let's pick the right three or four. What are your strongest packaging and branding projects? The ones where you're most proud of both the design outcome and the strategic thinking behind it.

Okay so I have a sake label project that's really beautiful — custom kanji lettering, the whole thing. Then a cafe identity I did last spring, full system with menus and signage. A tea packaging line that turned out amazing. And then some food packaging work that's solid but not as visually exciting. The cafe and the sake label are probably my strongest from a storytelling perspective.

Go with the sake label, the cafe identity, and the tea packaging — that gives you range within packaging and branding, and all three sound like they have a story to tell. Three strong ones is plenty.

For each case study, here's the structure agencies want to see:

1. **The Brief** — Who's the client, what did they need, what was the constraint or challenge? (2-3 sentences)
2. **The Process** — How did you approach it? Show sketches, iterations, strategic decisions. This is where agencies see your thinking.
3. **The Outcome** — Final work, beautifully presented. Mockups in context (on shelf, in space, in hand) if possible.
4. **The Impact** — Did sales go up? Did the client get press? Even a client quote works here.

Do you have process shots and mockups for those three projects?

I have process shots for the sake label — I screenshot everything obsessively. The cafe one I have some early sketches and the final photography. The tea packaging I have the final work but I'd need to make mockups. Honestly the mockup part stresses me out because I keep fiddling with them instead of finishing

With six days and a tendency to fiddle, time-boxing is your friend. Here's a rough schedule:

**Days 1-2 (today and tomorrow):** Write all three case study narratives — the brief, process, outcome sections. Just the text. Get it done before you touch any visuals, because the writing is the part people procrastinate on most.

**Days 3-4:** Compile the visual assets. Sake label: curate your best process shots into a clean sequence. Cafe: scan or photograph the sketches, pair with finals. Tea packaging: make the mockups — give yourself a 2-hour time limit and use a good template so you're not building from scratch.

**Day 5:** Assemble everything on your site. Polish the layout.

**Day 6:** Buffer day for tweaks and a fresh-eyes review.

For mockups: use a pre-made template pack. Are you working in Photoshop for mockups, or something else?

Figma for the layout and Photoshop for the mockups yeah. I have some mockup templates already I just need to drop the artwork in. The two-hour limit is a good idea because I will absolutely spend eight hours on a mockup if nobody stops me. The writing is the part I'm dreading though, I'm not a writer at all

Most designers dread the writing. Here's a trick: don't write it like an essay. Write it the way you'd explain the project to another designer at a bar. Keep it casual and specific.

For example, for the sake label:
- Don't write: "The objective was to create a premium label system that communicated heritage and craftsmanship."
- Do write: "The brewery wanted something that felt premium but not stuffy — traditional craft, modern shelf appeal. The custom kanji lettering was the centerpiece."

Keep each section to 2-4 sentences max. Agencies skim case studies — they're looking at the visuals and scanning the text for evidence that you think strategically. You don't need paragraphs.

Want to draft the sake label brief section right now? I can help you tighten it up.

yeah okay let me try. so the brewery is a small independent place, been around for like 40 years. they wanted new labels for their core lineup because the old ones looked dated. they're competing against bigger brands on shelf so they needed to stand out but still feel authentic to who they are. I spent a lot of time on the lettering because I wanted the kanji to have the same visual weight and rhythm as the latin text on the back label. that was the biggest challenge honestly

That's basically already written — you just need to clean it up slightly. Here's a tightened version:

**Brief:** "A 40-year-old independent sake brewery needed a label refresh for their core lineup. The existing labels felt dated and were getting lost against bigger brands on shelf. They wanted something that stood out but still felt authentic."

**Process/Challenge:** "The biggest design challenge was the lettering — I hand-drew the kanji so it would carry the same visual weight and rhythm as the latin text on the back label. Getting that balance right between the two scripts took the most iteration."

See? You already had it, you just didn't realize it was writing. Do the same thing for the cafe and tea projects — talk through it naturally and then trim. You'll have all three briefs done in under an hour.
okay that actually doesn't look that hard when you put it that way. I think I was overthinking it — like I thought it needed to sound professional and impressive but keeping it direct is better right

Direct is always better for case studies. Agencies read dozens of portfolios — the ones that communicate clearly and quickly stand out more than the ones with inflated marketing language. Show you can think, show you can execute, keep it concise. Your work speaks for itself.

One more thing: when you present on the 20th, they'll probably ask "walk me through this project." Practice saying it out loud once or twice — not memorized, just comfortable. If you can talk through the sake label the way you just explained it to me, you'll be fine.

the talking through it part I'm actually okay with, it's just the writing that gets me. Okay I'm going to start the narratives tonight. It's almost midnight so honestly this is when I do my best work anyway. Thank you for the structure — having a day-by-day plan makes this feel way less terrifying

Midnight is as good a time as any to write about your own work — no distractions, no client emails coming in. Stick to the schedule, use the two-hour mockup limit, and don't reorganize your desk instead of writing. You've got this. Good luck on the 20th.
